title: Construct BT from Postorder and Inorder

prerequisites: Construct BST from Preorder and Inorder
Construct BT from Preorder and Inorder

statement.markdown:
Given two integer arrays `inorder` and `postorder` where `inorder` is the inorder traversal of a binary tree and `postorder` is the postorder traversal of the same tree, construct and return the binary tree.

You may assume that duplicates do not exist in the tree.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains a single integer N, the number of nodes in the tree.
The next N lines contain the elements of the inorder traversal.
The next line contains a single integer representing the size of the postorder traversal (which is also N).
The next N lines contain the elements of the postorder traversal.
statement.outputFormat: The preorder traversal of the constructed binary tree, with node values separated by spaces.

constraints.description: The number of nodes in the tree.
Node values in the inorder array
Node values in the postorder array

examples.explanation: The tree has root 1, left child 2 (with children 4 and 5), and right child 3. Preorder traversal is 1 2 4 5 3.
The tree has root 1 and left child 2. Preorder traversal is 1 2.

hints.text: The problem involves constructing a binary tree from its inorder and postorder traversals. Recall that inorder traversal visits the left subtree, the current node, and then the right subtree, while postorder traversal visits the left subtree, the right subtree, and then the current node.
The last element of the postorder array is the root of the tree. You can use this information to divide both the inorder and postorder arrays into left and right subtrees.

To recursively construct the tree, find the index of the root node in the inorder array. This index will help you split both the inorder and postorder arrays into subarrays representing the left and right subtrees.

editorial.title: Constructing a Binary Tree from Postorder and Inorder Traversals
editorial.intuition: The problem can be solved efficiently by observing that the postorder traversal gives us the root of the tree (last element) and the inorder traversal gives us the position of the root in the tree, allowing us to divide the tree into left and right subtrees recursively.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve generating all possible binary trees and checking which one matches the given postorder and inorder traversals. However, this approach is extremely inefficient, with a time complexity of O(4^N) or worse, because there are exponentially many possible binary trees of N nodes.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach uses a recursive divide-and-conquer strategy, taking advantage of the properties of inorder and postorder traversals.
editorial.optimalSolution: By using a hash map to store the indices of inorder elements, we can find the position of the root in O(1) time and recursively construct the left and right subtrees.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by observing that it correctly reconstructs the tree: the postorder traversal ensures that the root is always processed last, and the inorder traversal ensures that the left subtree nodes are processed before the right subtree nodes. The use of a hash map for inorder indices ensures efficient lookup and division of the tree into subtrees.
editorial.dryRun: For example, given inorder = [9, 3, 15, 20, 7] and postorder = [9, 15, 7, 20, 3], the solution constructs the binary tree as follows:       3     /   \    9     20   / \   15  7
commonMistakes: Not using a hash map to store inorder indices, leading to O(N) time complexity for finding the root's position in the inorder traversal.
Incorrectly handling the base case of the recursion, leading to errors in tree construction.
Not updating the postorder index correctly during recursion, leading to incorrect tree construction.

functionSignature.java: public TreeNode buildTree(int[] inorder, int[] postorder);
functionSignature.python: def buildTree(self, inorder: List[int], postorder: List[int]) -> TreeNode:
